programs.name: MI Health Link
programs.tagline: Combined Medicare and Medicaid care with no co-pays for qualifying seniors in select Michigan counties.

localEntities.name: Regional ICOs varying by eligible counties

programs.intro:
If your parent has both Medicare and Medicaid and lives in specific Michigan counties, MI Health Link eliminates co-pays and deductibles while coordinating all their care under one team. Because all medical services, medications, dental, vision, and home care are managed by one Integrated Care Organization (ICO), your parent won't need to navigate between multiple providers or pay out-of-pocket costs for covered services.

This program is only available in Barry, Berrien, Branch, Calhoun, Cass, Kalamazoo, Macomb, St. Joseph, Van Buren, Wayne counties, and all Upper Peninsula counties. Your parent must already qualify for full Medicaid (not spend-down) and have Medicare. If they need home care services, they must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management).

summary: Must have Medicare and full Medicaid
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: For home care services: must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (assessed through Michigan's LOCD tool, which evaluates daily living skills, cognition, and behavior)
otherRequirements: Must have both Medicare and full Medicaid (no spend-down)
Live in Barry, Berrien, Branch, Calhoun, Cass, Kalamazoo, Macomb, St. Joseph, Van Buren, Wayne, or Upper Peninsula counties
Cannot be in PACE or MI Choice programs
Cannot live in state-operated veteran's home

applicationGuide.summary: Eligible individuals receive enrollment letters automatically from Michigan ENROLLS, or you can apply through local MDHHS offices.

steps.title: Check if your parent received an enrollment letter
steps.description: Michigan ENROLLS automatically sends enrollment options to eligible dual Medicare-Medicaid recipients in covered counties.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of Medicare and Medicaid eligibility, county residency, and medical records if seeking home care services.

steps.title: Complete consent forms
steps.description: Fill out Authorization to Disclose Protected Health Information (DCH-1183) and Behavioral Health Consent Form (MDHHS-5515).

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply through your local MDHHS office or contact MI ENROLLS for enrollment assistance.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent is currently in PACE or MI Choice, they must leave those programs to join MI Health Link.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Additional vehicles beyond one primary car
Real estate other than primary home
excluded: Burial funds and prepaid funeral plans
Life insurance with cash value under limits

categories.name: Medical Records
items: Medical records supporting need for home care (if applicable)

categories.name: Required Forms
items: Authorization to Disclose Protected Health Information (DCH-1183)

contentSections.text: Your parent's Medicare Part D drug plan will be replaced by MI Health Link's prescription coverage. They cannot keep both.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is exempt from asset limits as long as equity doesn't exceed $730,000. This applies whether your parent lives there, intends to return, or if a spouse or dependent child lives there.

faqs.question: What happens to Dad's current Medicare Part D prescription plan?
faqs.answer: MI Health Link replaces Medicare Part D with its own prescription coverage. Your father cannot keep both plans and must switch to MI Health Link's pharmacy benefits.

faqs.question: Can Mom get paid family caregiver services through this program?
faqs.answer: Yes, MI Health Link can provide personal care services including paid family caregivers, but only after your mother meets Nursing Facility Level of Care through a clinical assessment of her daily living needs.

faqs.question: What if Dad has Medicaid spend-down?
faqs.answer: He cannot qualify for MI Health Link. The program only accepts people with full Medicaid benefits, not those who meet Medicaid through spend-down of excess income.

faqs.question: If Mom is currently in MI Choice, can she switch to MI Health Link?
faqs.answer: She must leave MI Choice to join MI Health Link. She cannot be enrolled in both programs simultaneously.

faqs.question: Will there be co-pays for doctor visits and medications?
faqs.answer: No co-pays or deductibles for in-network services including medications, doctor visits, dental care, vision care, and behavioral health services.

programs.name: MI Choice Medicaid Waiver
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily help at home but wants to avoid a nursing home, this program covers in-home care, meals, and medical equipment.

localEntities.name: Regional AAAs and partners (e.g., AAANM, CareWell Services, The Senior Alliance, Area Agency on Aging).

programs.intro:
If your parent needs nursing-home-level care but wants to stay home, MI Choice covers comprehensive in-home services that would otherwise cost thousands monthly. The program provides personal care, adult day programs, home-delivered meals, medical equipment, respite care for family caregivers, and care coordination — all the supports needed to live safely at home instead of moving to a facility.

Your parent must be 65+ (or disabled 18+) with monthly income below $2,901 and assets under $9,950. Most importantly, they must qualify for nursing facility level of care through a clinical assessment — meaning they need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or have significant cognitive impairment. Because this replaces expensive nursing home care, participants receive individualized service plans worth potentially $50,000+ annually in care coordination, personal care, and medical supports.

summary: Must use supports coordinator plus one other service
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must qualify for Nursing Facility Level of Care through in-person assessment of activities of daily living, cognitive abilities, and behavior — essentially needing daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or having significant memory/safety issues
otherRequirements: Must need supports coordination plus at least one other waiver service
Cannot have given away assets in past 60 months without penalty

applicationGuide.summary: Call your regional Area Agency on Aging for phone screening, followed by in-home assessment if you qualify.

steps.title: Call your regional agency
steps.description: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging for initial phone screening about your parent's care needs and income

steps.title: Complete phone screening
steps.description: Agency determines if your parent meets basic income, asset, and care level requirements

steps.title: Schedule in-home assessment
steps.description: If qualified, a nurse or social worker visits your parent's home to conduct Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment

steps.title: Develop service plan
steps.description: If approved, supports coordinator creates individualized plan covering specific services your parent needs

applicationGuide.processingTime: Timeline varies by region after initial screening
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible if program at capacity in your region
applicationGuide.tip: Have your parent's medical records ready — the assessment focuses on specific daily care needs, not just a dementia diagnosis alone

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts Toward $9,950 Limit
included: Assets given away in past 60 months (penalty applies)
excluded: Primary residence (but subject to estate recovery after death)
Burial funds up to certain limits

categories.name: Medical
items: Recent medical records showing care needs
Documentation of help needed with daily activities

contentSections.text: Income limit is $2,901 per person, not per household. If your parents are married and both need services, each spouse is assessed individually for income eligibility.
Your parent's home is protected while receiving benefits, but Michigan can recover costs from the home's value after death unless a spouse or disabled child still lives there.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for MI Choice?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary residence doesn't count toward the $9,950 asset limit while she receives benefits. However, Michigan can recover program costs from the home's value after death through Medicaid Estate Recovery, unless a spouse or disabled child still lives there.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over $2,901 per month?
faqs.answer: He won't qualify for MI Choice. The income limit is firm at $2,901 monthly per person in 2025. This limit adjusts annually with federal rates, so check again next year if he's close to the threshold.

faqs.question: Will having dementia automatically qualify Mom for this program?
faqs.answer: No. A dementia diagnosis alone isn't enough. She must pass a full Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment showing she needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or has significant safety/behavior issues requiring supervision.

faqs.question: Can I get paid as Mom's caregiver through this program?
faqs.answer: Yes, MI Choice can pay family members to provide personal care services. The supports coordinator includes this in your mom's individualized service plan if it meets her assessed needs.

faqs.question: What happens if there's a waitlist in our area?
faqs.answer: You'll be placed on a waiting list after completing the initial screening. Waitlist times vary by region and your parent's priority level based on care needs. Stay in contact with your regional agency for updates.

faqs.question: What if Dad gave money to grandchildren last year?
faqs.answer: Any assets given away or sold below fair market value in the past 60 months create a penalty period where he can't receive benefits. The penalty length depends on the amount given away. Be honest about all transfers during the application process.

programs.name: Michigan Program for All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing home care but wants to stay home, PACE provides all medical care and daily support under one program.

localEntities.name: PACE of Southwest Michigan (Berrien, Cass, Van Buren Counties)
PACE Northeast Michigan (Alcona, Alpena, Presque Isle, Montmorency Counties; parts of Iosco)

programs.intro:
If your parent qualifies for nursing home care but wants to stay home, Michigan PACE provides all their medical care, prescriptions, therapy, meals, and daily support through one coordinated team. Instead of juggling separate doctors, insurance claims, and care providers, your parent gets everything from primary care to transportation to adult day services — with no copays if they have Medicaid.

PACE is only available in specific areas of Michigan and requires your parent to be 55+ and certified as needing nursing home level of care (daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management). Because PACE covers all care under one program, your parent cannot see outside doctors or specialists without PACE approval — the program becomes their complete healthcare system.

If your parent has both Medicare and Medicaid, PACE costs nothing. If they only have Medicare, they pay a monthly premium based on their income and assets, which PACE will help calculate during enrollment.

summary: Able to live safely at home with support
Cannot have MIChoice waiver or HMO
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified by the state as meeting nursing home level of care (needing daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management)
otherRequirements: Live in approved PACE service area
Able to live safely in community with PACE support
Cannot be enrolled in MIChoice waiver or HMO

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local PACE provider directly for an intake assessment — no application form needed.

steps.title: Find your PACE provider
steps.description: Use the provider directory below to find which PACE organization serves your parent's area. Each provider has exclusive service areas.

steps.title: Call for intake assessment
steps.description: The PACE provider will schedule an in-person assessment at their center to evaluate your parent's care needs and eligibility.

steps.title: Complete enrollment assessment
steps.description: If eligible, PACE's interdisciplinary team creates a personalized care plan covering all your parent's medical and support needs.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid (if needed)
steps.description: PACE staff will help your parent apply for Medicaid to eliminate all costs. If not Medicaid-eligible, they'll calculate the monthly premium.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible, varies by region and provider
applicationGuide.tip: Bring all medical records to show your parent's care needs — this helps speed up the nursing home level of care assessment.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits for Free PACE (Medicaid Eligibility)
excluded: Burial funds up to reasonable amount

offices.name: Ascension Living PACE Michigan
offices.address: Check ascensionliving.org for specific service areas

offices.name: PACE of Southwest Michigan
offices.address: Serves Berrien, Cass, and Van Buren Counties — paceswmi.org

offices.name: PACE Northeast Michigan
offices.address: Serves Alcona, Alpena, Presque Isle, Montmorency Counties and parts of Iosco County

offices.name: Senior Care Partners P.A.C.E.
offices.address: Mapped service area — contact for zip code eligibility

categories.name: Financial (for Medicaid)
items: Pay stubs or Social Security award letter

contentSections.text: PACE is not available statewide. You must live in a specific PACE provider's service area. Check with providers directly using your parent's zip code before starting the application process.
Your parent must receive ALL medical care through PACE (except emergencies). They cannot see their current doctors or specialists unless PACE approves it. Consider this carefully before enrolling.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her current doctors if she enrolls in PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE requires your mom to receive all medical care through their team of providers. She cannot see outside doctors or specialists unless PACE specifically approves and arranges it. This is a major change from traditional Medicare.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is too high for Medicaid but she can't afford private insurance?
faqs.answer: PACE offers a private pay option based on your mom's income and assets. The PACE provider will calculate her monthly premium during the enrollment process. Many families find this more affordable than coordinating multiple services separately.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your mom's primary home doesn't count toward Medicaid asset limits, regardless of value. PACE is designed to help people stay in their homes instead of moving to nursing facilities.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom needs to move to a different area of Michigan?
faqs.answer: Each PACE provider serves exclusive geographic areas. If your mom moves outside her current PACE service area, she would need to disenroll and find new healthcare coverage. Check service boundaries before any moves.

faqs.question: How is this different from other home care programs like MIChoice?
faqs.answer: PACE provides all medical care plus home support services through one program. MIChoice only covers home care services — you still need separate doctors and insurance. Your mom cannot be enrolled in both programs at the same time.

faqs.question: What if there's a waiting list for PACE in our area?
faqs.answer: Waiting lists vary by provider and region. Contact your local PACE provider to ask about current wait times and get on their list. Continue your mom's current care while waiting, as PACE cannot guarantee enrollment timing.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (MSP) - QMB, SLMB, QI
programs.tagline: If your parent has Medicare and limited income, they may get $200–$2,400+ annually in premium and cost-sharing help.
programs.intro:
If your parent has Medicare and earns less than $1,585/month ($2,135 for couples), Michigan's Medicare Savings Programs can pay their Medicare premiums and potentially all their Medicare out-of-pocket costs. Three programs exist with different income limits and benefits: QMB covers all Medicare premiums and copays, SLMB covers just the Part B premium ($202.90/month in 2026), and QI covers the Part B premium plus prescription drug assistance.

These programs work alongside Medicare — your parent keeps their current Medicare coverage and doctors. Because the state pays Medicare costs directly, your parent won't see bills for covered services. The main catch: QI operates on first-come, first-served basis with limited funding, and QI recipients must reapply every year.
programs.savingsSource: Medicare Part B premium plus potential deductibles and copays

summary: Has Medicare Part A and/or Part B
Must not receive other Medicaid (QI only)
otherRequirements: SLMB and QI require premium-free Medicare Part A
QI not available if receiving other Medicaid benefits

applicationGuide.summary: Call Michigan's Medicaid line at 800-642-3195 — approval takes up to 45 days.

steps.title: Call Michigan Medicaid
steps.description: Call 800-642-3195 to request Medicare Savings Program application forms and get specific document requirements.

steps.title: Gather financial documents
steps.description: Collect Medicare cards, Social Security award letters, bank statements, and any other income documentation from the past 3 months.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Return completed application with required documents to MDHHS. For QI program, apply early in the year due to limited funding.

steps.title: Follow up on processing
steps.description: QMB decisions take up to 45 days. SLMB can be retroactive up to 3 months if eligible. QI coverage begins from the approval calendar year.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Up to 45 days for QMB; timelines for SLMB and QI not specified
applicationGuide.waitlist: QI program operates on first-come, first-served basis with limited annual funding
applicationGuide.tip: Apply for QI early in the calendar year — funding runs out and there's a waitlist.

rows.coverage: Part B premium + prescription drug help

categories.name: Medicare Documentation
items: Medicare card (Part A and/or Part B)

contentSections.text: QI program has limited funding and operates first-come, first-served. Apply early in the calendar year to avoid the waitlist.
If your parent receives SSI-related Medicaid, they automatically qualify for QMB — no separate application needed.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her current Medicare doctors if she gets MSP?
faqs.answer: Yes. MSP works with her existing Medicare coverage — same doctors, same Medicare benefits. The state just pays the bills Medicare doesn't cover.

faqs.question: What happens if her income is just over the QMB limit but under SLMB?
faqs.answer: She can still qualify for SLMB, which pays her Medicare Part B premium ($202.90/month in 2026). She just won't get help with deductibles and copays like QMB provides.

faqs.question: Does her house count toward the $9,950 asset limit?
faqs.answer: The research doesn't specify which assets are exempt, but typically the home where your parent lives doesn't count. Call 800-642-3195 to confirm what assets count in Michigan.

faqs.question: If she qualifies, when does the coverage start?
faqs.answer: QMB starts the month after approval with no retroactive coverage. SLMB can go back up to 3 months if she was eligible. QI starts from January of the approval year (or approval month if after January).

faqs.question: What if she doesn't have premium-free Medicare Part A?
faqs.answer: She can still get QMB, which will pay her Part A premiums. But SLMB and QI require premium-free Part A — if she's paying for Part A, those programs aren't available.

faqs.question: How often does she need to reapply?
faqs.answer: QMB and SLMB don't require annual reapplication. QI requires reapplication every year, and previous QI recipients get priority in the funding queue.
